topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the ballroom was thick with the scent of lilies and expensive perfume. I was nursing a lukewarm champagne, trying to appear engaged in a conversation about the questionable catering choices, when I spotted him near the punch bowl. It was him. Alex. He was laughing, a sound I hadn't heard in years, his smile reaching those familiar, unguarded corners of his eyes. My hand froze halfway to my glass. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that felt too loud in the ambient chatter. I watched as he turned, and for a split second, the world seemed to tilt on its axis. He caught my gaze, and the easy warmth in his expression instantly cooled, replaced by a flicker of something unreadable—a swift, almost panicked adjustment of his posture. I immediately looked down at my napkin, suddenly intensely aware of the way my fingers were trembling slightly.

[story 2]

"Oh, look who decided to grace us with their presence," Sarah chirped, nudging me forward. I followed her gaze, and there he was. Alex. Standing by the archway, looking impossibly composed in a tailored suit. My internal monologue stuttered. *Wait. Is that really him?* My stomach did a strange flip, a churning sensation that made my throat tighten. I tried to focus on the intricate embroidery on the tablecloth, but my peripheral vision kept snagging on him. When our eyes met across the crowded room, there was a palpable silence between us, a sudden vacuum where familiarity used to reside. He offered a small, tight nod, a gesture so brief it felt like a deliberate dismissal. I felt a distinct pressure building behind my sternum, a strange, unwelcome weight settling over my shoulders. I took a slow, deliberate sip of my drink, trying to regulate the sudden, erratic rhythm of my heartbeat.

[story 3]

I was deep in conversation with Mark, dissecting the merits of modern architecture, when the music shifted, and suddenly, my focus snapped. Across the room, by the towering floral arrangements, stood Alex. He was talking animatedly with a group of people I vaguely recognized. Every muscle in my body seemed to lock up. I stopped mid-sentence, my mouth slightly agape. It wasn't just recognition; it was a complete recalibration of everything I thought I knew about the space. A strange, cold clarity washed over me, stripping away the pleasant haze of the celebration. I watched him for a long moment, cataloging every subtle shift in his expression—a slight frown here, a momentary glance away there. When he finally turned toward my general vicinity, there was no acknowledgment, just a smooth, practiced turn of his head that sent a distinct shiver down my spine. I found myself gripping my wine glass so tightly my knuckles turned white.
